title: Ordered Set Partitions, Generalized Coinvariant Algebras, and the Delta Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper defines generalized coinvariant algebras R_{n,k} = Q[x1,...,xn]/<x1^k,...,xn^k,e_n,e_{n-1},...,e_{n-k+1}> for 1 ≤ k ≤ n and proves that their algebraic and representation-theoretic structure is governed by ordered set partitions of [n] with k blocks. Its main results give the Hilbert series, ungraded S_n-module structure, standard monomial bases, Gröbner bases, generalized Artin and Garsia-Stanton bases, and a full graded Frobenius formula. The graded Frobenius character is identified with the t = 0 specialization of the combinatorial side of the Delta Conjecture, after applying q-reversal and the involution ω, and it has explicit Schur and dual Hall-Littlewood expansions. For the original hook-coefficient problem, the most directly useful outputs are the Schur expansion in terms of standard Young tableaux and q-binomials, the ordered multiset partition/Gessel fundamental expansions, and the repeated use of exterior/antisymmetrization operators e_j^⊥, which are naturally connected to hook-shaped Schur phenomena.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1, Equations (2.8)-(2.9): Mahonian statistics on ordered set partitions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let OP_{n,k} be the set of ordered set partitions σ = (B1 | ... | Bk) of [n] into k ordered blocks. For σ in OP_{n,k}, inv(σ) is the number of pairs i < j such that i is the minimum element of some block B_m, j lies in a block B_l, and l < m. Define coinv(σ) = (n-k)(k-1) + binom(k,2) - inv(σ). The paper also uses a major-index statistic maj(σ), defined by viewing σ as an ascent-starred permutation, and its complement comaj(σ) = (n-k)(k-1) + binom(k,2) - maj(σ). If Stir_q(n,k) is defined recursively by Stir_q(n,k) = Stir_q(n-1,k-1) + [k]_q Stir_q(n-1,k), with Stir_q(1,1)=1 and Stir_q(1,k)=0 for k>1, then the cited results of Steingrímsson and Remmel-Wilson give ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{inv(σ)} = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{maj(σ)} = [k]!_q Stir_q(n,k). Consequently ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{coinv(σ)} = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{comaj(σ)} = rev_q([k]!_q Stir_q(n,k)), where rev_q reverses a polynomial in q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls these identities rather than reproving the equidistribution from scratch. The complementary formulas follow immediately from the explicit maximum value (n-k)(k-1)+binom(k,2) of inv and maj on OP_{n,k}. Later in the paper, the authors also use a direct insertion viewpoint: an ordered set partition of size n can be built from one of size n-1 either by inserting n into an existing block, called star insertion, or by inserting n as a singleton block, called bar insertion. These insertions change coinv by controlled amounts, giving the recursion P_{n,k}(q) = [k]_q(P_{n-1,k}(q)+q^{n-k}P_{n-1,k-1}(q)) for the coinv generating function, matching the q-Stirling recursion after multiplication by [k]!_q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (2.19)-(2.23): Delta-specialization and ordered multiset partition expansions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Risen_{n,k}(x;q,t) and Val_{n,k}(x;q,t) denote the two combinatorial sides of the Delta Conjecture in the paper's indexing convention. Prior work cited in the paper gives Risen_{n,k}(x;q,0) = Risen_{n,k}(x;0,q) = Val_{n,k}(x;q,0) = Val_{n,k}(x;0,q); call this common symmetric function C_{n,k}(x;q). Let D_{n,k}(x;q) = rev_q(ω C_{n,k}(x;q)), where ω is the usual involution on symmetric functions sending s_λ to s_{λ'} and rev_q reverses the q-polynomial. For a weak composition γ of n, let OP_{γ,k} be the set of ordered multiset partitions of content γ with k blocks. Then C_{n,k}(x;q) can be written in any of the four forms ∑_{|γ|=n} ∑_{μ in OP_{γ,k}} q^{inv(μ)} x^γ, ∑ q^{maj(μ)} x^γ, ∑ q^{dinv(μ)} x^γ, or ∑ q^{minimaj(μ)} x^γ, using the corresponding ordered multiset partition statistics. Equivalently, if F_{n,D}(x) is Gessel's fundamental quasisymmetric function, rword(σ) is the reading word of an ordered set partition σ, revword(σ) is its reverse reading word, and iDes(π) is the descent set of π^{-1}, then D_{n,k}(x;q) = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{coinv(σ)} F_{n,iDes(revword(σ))}(x).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The four ordered multiset partition formulas are quoted from Haglund-Remmel-Wilson, Wilson, and Rhoades. For the fundamental quasisymmetric expansion, group ordered multiset partitions by the standardization of their reading word. The reading word is chosen so that inversion pairs are preserved under standardization, hence Val_{n,k}(x;q,0) = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{inv(σ)} F_{n,iDes(rword(σ))}(x). The standard identity for the involution ω on a symmetric function expressed in Gessel fundamentals sends the summand indexed by a word to the summand indexed by the reversed word. Finally q-reversal changes inv into coinv because coinv is the complement of inv with respect to the common maximum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1: elementary-homogeneous vanishing identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k ≤ n, let α1,...,αk be distinct rational numbers, and let β1,...,βn be rational numbers whose set of values is exactly {α1,...,αk}. Then for every integer r with n-k+1 ≤ r ≤ n, one has ∑_{j=0}^{r} (-1)^j e_{r-j}(β1,...,βn) h_j(α1,...,αk) = 0, where e_d and h_d are elementary and complete homogeneous symmetric polynomials.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The left-hand side is the coefficient of t^r in the rational function ∏_{i=1}^n(1+tβ_i) / ∏_{j=1}^k(1+tα_j). Because every α_j appears among the β_i, every denominator factor cancels a numerator factor. The resulting expression is a polynomial in t of degree n-k. Therefore all coefficients of t^r for r > n-k vanish, which is exactly the stated range.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4: skip-composition Demazure identity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix k ≤ n and a subset S = {s1 < ... < s_{n-k+1}} of [n]. Define the skip composition γ(S) = (γ1,...,γn) by γ_i = 0 if i is not in S and γ_{s_j} = s_j - j + 1; equivalently the skip monomial is x(S) = x_{s1}^{s1} x_{s2}^{s2-1} ... x_{s_{n-k+1}}^{s_{n-k+1}-(n-k)}. Let γ(S)^* denote reversal of the composition, and let overline{γ(S)^*} be obtained by subtracting 1 from every positive part of γ(S)^*. Let κ_δ(x1,...,xn) be the Demazure character indexed by the weak composition δ. Then κ_{γ(S)^*}(x1,...,xn) = ∑_λ (-1)^{|λ|} κ_{overline{γ(S)^*}-λ}(x1,...,xn) e_{n-k+1+|λ|}(x1,...,xn), where the sum is over all left-leaning collections λ of cells in the skyline diagram of overline{γ(S)^*}. Reversing variables gives κ_{γ(S)^*}(xn,...,x1) = ∑_λ (-1)^{|λ|} κ_{overline{γ(S)^*}-λ}(xn,...,x1) e_{n-k+1+|λ|}(x1,...,xn).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof encodes the right-hand side using labeled skyline diagrams called partisan skylines. A partisan skyline records cells retained from overline{γ(S)^*}, cells removed in a left-leaning set, cells added through a right-biased Pieri expansion for multiplying a Demazure character by an elementary symmetric function, and cells that are both removed and re-added. The Demazure dual Pieri rule of Haglund-Luoto-Mason-van Willigenburg expands e_d(x)κ_γ(x) as a sum of Demazure characters over right-biased cell additions. Thus the right-hand side is a signed sum over partisan skylines. The authors construct a sign-reversing, weight-preserving involution on all partisan skylines except one completely frozen skyline. The key local move changes the highest non-removed cell in the leftmost minimal unfrozen column from one label to another; two claims verify that the move preserves the partisan skyline conditions and is an involution. All terms cancel except the completely frozen skyline, whose weight is γ(S)^* and sign is +1, giving the identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6: detecting equality by all e_j^⊥
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let F(x) and G(x) be symmetric functions with equal constant terms. Then F(x) = G(x) if and only if e_j(x)^⊥ F(x) = e_j(x)^⊥ G(x) for every j ≥ 1, where e_j^⊥ is adjoint to multiplication by e_j under the Hall inner product.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The forward implication is immediate. Conversely, for every partition λ and every j ≥ 1, adjointness gives <F,e_j e_λ> = <e_j^⊥F,e_λ> = <e_j^⊥G,e_λ> = <G,e_j e_λ>. Together with equality of constant terms, this proves that F and G have the same inner products with every product e_μ. Since the elementary symmetric functions form a basis, F=G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7: e_j^⊥ recurrence for D_{n,k}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For positive integers k ≤ n and j ≥ 1, the symmetric functions D_{n,k}(x;q) defined by D_{n,k} = rev_q(ω C_{n,k}) satisfy e_j(x)^⊥ D_{n,k}(x;q) = q^{binom(j,2)} [k choose j]_q ∑_{m=max(1,k-j)}^{min(k,n-j)} q^{(k-m)(n-j-m)} [j choose k-m]_q D_{n-j,m}(x;q). Here [a choose b]_q is the q-binomial coefficient and terms outside the displayed bounds are absent.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof tests both sides against products e_{β2}(x)...e_{βp}(x), with β = (j,β2,...,βp) a composition of n. Via the Gessel fundamental expansion and superization identities, the scalar product with e_β counts ordered set partitions σ whose reverse reading word is a shuffle of specified decreasing blocks. The action of e_j^⊥ corresponds to separating off the first decreasing block, i.e. choosing how to add the j largest letters n-j+1,...,n to an ordered set partition of [n-j]. If r = k-m of those large letters become singleton blocks, the remaining j-r large letters are inserted into existing blocks, subject to the reverse reading order condition. Counting the additional coinversions gives q^{r(n-j-k+r)+binom(j,2)} [k choose r]_q [k-r choose j-r]_q, which is rewritten as q^{binom(j,2)}[k choose j]_q q^{(k-m)(n-j-m)}[j choose k-m]_q. Summing over m gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.9: bijection between ordered set partitions and nonskip monomials
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For k ≤ n, call a monomial m in Q[x1,...,xn] (n,k)-nonskip if x_i^k does not divide m for every i and x(S) does not divide m for every subset S of [n] with |S| = n-k+1, where x(S) is the skip monomial defined by x(S)=x_{s1}^{s1}x_{s2}^{s2-1}... for S={s1<...}. Let M_{n,k} be the set of all (n,k)-nonskip monomials. There is a bijection Ψ: OP_{n,k} → M_{n,k} such that deg Ψ(σ) = coinv(σ) for every σ in OP_{n,k}. Consequently |M_{n,k}| = |OP_{n,k}| and ∑_{m in M_{n,k}} q^{deg m} = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{coinv(σ)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The map Ψ is recursive. For n=1, send the unique ordered set partition to 1. Given σ in OP_{n,k}, remove n, and if n was not a singleton block then σ was obtained by star insertion into block i+1 of an element σ_bar in OP_{n-1,k}; set Ψ(σ)=Ψ(σ_bar)x_n^i. If n was a singleton block, then σ was obtained by bar insertion in position i+1 from σ_bar in OP_{n-1,k-1}. A technical lemma gives a unique subset S of [n-1] of size n-k such that x(S) divides m(S)Ψ(σ_bar), but no skip monomial of size n-k+1 divides it; set Ψ(σ)=Ψ(σ_bar)m(S)x_n^i. The authors prove by induction that the output is nonskip. The inverse Φ removes the x_n exponent. If the remaining monomial lies in M_{n-1,k}, perform the corresponding star insertion; otherwise a unique skip divisor x(S) of size n-k exists, divide by m(S), and perform bar insertion. The recursive maps undo each other. The degree increments i for star insertion and n-k+i for bar insertion match exactly the coinv increments under the corresponding insertion operations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.10: Hilbert series of R_{n,k}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let R_{n,k} = Q[x1,...,xn]/I_{n,k}, where I_{n,k} = <x1^k,...,xn^k,e_n(x),e_{n-1}(x),...,e_{n-k+1}(x)>. Then Hilb(R_{n,k};q) = ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{coinv(σ)} = rev_q([k]!_q Stir_q(n,k)). In particular dim R_{n,k} = |OP_{n,k}| = k! Stir(n,k).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose distinct rationals α1,...,αk and let Y_{n,k} be the finite point set of all n-tuples whose coordinates use exactly the values α1,...,αk. There is an S_n-equivariant bijection Y_{n,k} ↔ OP_{n,k}. Let I(Y_{n,k}) be the vanishing ideal and T(Y_{n,k}) the ideal generated by top-degree components of nonzero polynomials in I(Y_{n,k}). The Garsia-Procesi point-set method gives dim Q[x]/T(Y_{n,k}) = |Y_{n,k}| and the same ungraded S_n-module as Q[Y_{n,k}]. Lemma 3.1 gives e_r(x) in T(Y_{n,k}) for r ≥ n-k+1, while ∏_{a=1}^k(x_i-α_a) gives x_i^k in T(Y_{n,k}); hence I_{n,k} ⊆ T(Y_{n,k}). On the other hand, Lemma 3.4 puts certain reverse Demazure characters in I_{n,k}, and Lemma 3.5 identifies their lexicographic leading terms as the skip monomials x(S); together with x_i^k, this shows the standard monomials for I_{n,k} are contained in M_{n,k}. The inclusions of standard monomial sets BT ⊆ BI ⊆ M_{n,k} and the cardinality equality |BT|=|OP_{n,k}|=|M_{n,k}| force equality throughout, so I_{n,k}=T(Y_{n,k}). The Hilbert series is then the degree generating function of M_{n,k}, which equals the coinv generating function by Theorem 4.9.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.11: ungraded S_n-module structure of R_{n,k}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: As ungraded S_n-modules, R_{n,k} is isomorphic to Q[OP_{n,k}], the permutation representation on ordered set partitions of [n] into k blocks. Equivalently, Frob(R_{n,k}) = ∑_{λ partition of n, ℓ(λ)=k} multinomial(k; m1(λ),...,mn(λ)) h_λ(x), where m_i(λ) is the multiplicity of i as a part of λ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the point-set model from Theorem 4.10. The quotient Q[x]/T(Y_{n,k}) is isomorphic, as an ungraded S_n-module, to Q[Y_{n,k}], and Y_{n,k} is S_n-equivariantly identified with OP_{n,k}. The dimension argument in Theorem 4.10 proves I_{n,k}=T(Y_{n,k}), so R_{n,k}=Q[x]/I_{n,k} has the same ungraded module. The Frobenius formula follows by decomposing OP_{n,k} according to the unordered block-size partition λ; for each λ of length k, the corresponding permutation representation has Frobenius h_λ and occurs with the number of orderings of equal-sized blocks, namely k!/(m1(λ)!...mn(λ)!).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.13: generalized Artin basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: An (n,k)-staircase is a shuffle of the sequence (0,1,...,k-1) with n-k additional copies of k-1. Let A_{n,k} be the set of monomials x1^{a1}...xn^{an} whose exponent vector (a1,...,an) is componentwise at most some (n,k)-staircase. Then A_{n,k}=M_{n,k}, the set of nonskip monomials, and A_{n,k} descends to a monomial basis of R_{n,k}. For k=n this recovers the classical Artin basis of the ordinary coinvariant algebra.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof of Theorem 4.10 already shows M_{n,k} is the standard monomial basis of R_{n,k}, so it remains to identify it with A_{n,k}. Any monomial bounded by an (n,k)-staircase is directly checked to avoid every forbidden divisor x_i^k and x(S), hence A_{n,k} ⊆ M_{n,k}. For the reverse containment, use the recursive bijection Ψ from Theorem 4.9. If σ is obtained by star-inserting n into an element of OP_{n-1,k}, then Ψ(σ)=Ψ(σ_bar)x_n^i with i≤k-1; appending k-1 to a bounding (n-1,k)-staircase gives a bounding (n,k)-staircase. If σ is obtained by bar insertion, then Ψ(σ)=Ψ(σ_bar)m(S)x_n^i for a set S of size n-k; define an (n,k)-staircase by putting k-1 in the positions S and n, and the remaining values 0,1,...,k-2 elsewhere. The nonskip condition prevents any exponent outside S∪{n} from exceeding the corresponding staircase value.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.14: lexicographic Gröbner basis of I_{n,k}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let < be lexicographic order on Q[x1,...,xn]. A Gröbner basis for I_{n,k} consists of the variable powers x1^k,...,xn^k together with the reverse Demazure characters κ_{γ(S)^*}(xn,...,x1) for all subsets S of [n-1] of size n-k+1, where γ(S) is the skip composition and γ(S)^* is its reversal. If k < n, this Gröbner basis is reduced. When k=n, the corresponding reduced Gröbner basis for the classical invariant ideal is {h_i(x_i,x_{i+1},...,x_n): 1≤i≤n}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 3.4 shows that each listed reverse Demazure character belongs to I_{n,k}, because it is expressed as a combination of elementary generators e_r(x) with r≥n-k+1. Lemma 3.5 proves that its lexicographic leading monomial is the skip monomial x(S); the variable powers give leading terms x_i^k. Theorem 4.13 and the dimension computation show that the monomials not divisible by these leading terms are exactly the standard monomial basis of R_{n,k}. Therefore the listed polynomials generate the initial ideal and form a Gröbner basis. Reducedness for k<n follows from the additional part of Lemma 3.5: no leading monomial from one listed polynomial divides any monomial appearing in another listed polynomial, and all leading coefficients are 1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.1 and Theorem 5.3: generalized Garsia-Stanton basis
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a permutation π=π1...πn in S_n, let Des(π) be its descent set, maj(π)=∑_{i in Des(π)} i, asc(π)=n-1-|Des(π)|, and define the classical Garsia-Stanton monomial gs_π = ∏_{i in Des(π)} x_{π1}x_{π2}...x_{πi}. Lemma 5.1 states that ∑_{σ in OP_{n,k}} q^{comaj(σ)} = ∑_{π in S_n} q^{maj(π)} [asc(π) choose n-k]_q. Define G S_{n,k} to be the set of monomials gs_π x_{π1}^{i1}x_{π2}^{i2}...x_{π,n-k}^{i_{n-k}}, where π in S_n satisfies des(π)<k and the exponents satisfy k-des(π) > i1 ≥ i2 ≥ ... ≥ i_{n-k} ≥ 0. Theorem 5.3 states that G S_{n,k} descends to a basis of R_{n,k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 5.1, view an ordered set partition as an ascent-starred permutation (π,S), with |S|=n-k. For fixed π, choosing the star set S among ascents is equivalent to choosing a partition inside an (n-k) by (asc(π)-n+k) rectangle, producing the q-binomial [asc(π) choose n-k]_q; the definition of comaj contributes the factor q^{maj(π)}. For Theorem 5.3, the generating function of the proposed monomials matches the right side of Lemma 5.1 and therefore has at most dim R_{n,k} terms. To prove spanning, use the Adin-Brenti-Roichman straightening relation for any monomial m: m = gs_{π(m)} e_{μ(m)}(x) - lower terms, where π(m) orders variables by decreasing exponent, μ(m) is determined from the exponent partition after subtracting the descent vector of π(m), and lower terms are smaller in a dominance/inversion partial order. Inducting on this order modulo I_{n,k}, if des(π(m)) ≥ k then gs_{π(m)} is killed by a variable power x_i^k, and if μ(m)_1 > n-k then e_{μ(m)_1}(x) is one of the elementary generators. In the remaining case, the monomial is either one of the specified generalized Garsia-Stanton monomials or is killed by a variable power. Thus G S_{n,k} spans; the Hilbert-count then gives a basis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.7 and Equation (6.2): auxiliary quotients R_{n,k,s}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For positive integers s ≤ k ≤ n, define I_{n,k,s} = <x1^k,...,xn^k,e_n(x),e_{n-1}(x),...,e_{n-s+1}(x)> and R_{n,k,s}=Q[x1,...,xn]/I_{n,k,s}. Let OP_{n,k,s} be the set of ordered set partitions of [n+k-s] into k blocks in which the distinguished large element n+i lies in block s+i for every 1≤i≤k-s; S_n acts only on the small letters 1,...,n. Let M_{n,k,s} be the set of monomials in Q[x1,...,xn] not divisible by any x_i^k and not divisible by any skip monomial x(S) with S⊆[n] and |S|=n-s+1. Then M_{n,k,s} is the standard monomial basis of R_{n,k,s}, dim R_{n,k,s}=|OP_{n,k,s}|, and as ungraded S_n-modules R_{n,k,s} is isomorphic to Q[OP_{n,k,s}].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof repeats the point-set and Gröbner strategy used for R_{n,k}. One introduces a point set Y_{n,k,s} in Q^{n+k-s} using k distinct scalars α1,...,αk, fixes the last k-s coordinates to α_{s+1},...,α_k, and requires all α_i to appear. This point set is S_n-equivariantly identified with OP_{n,k,s}. Passing to the top-degree ideal and then setting the last k-s variables to 0 gives an ideal T_{n,k,s}. Lemma 3.1 places e_r(x) for r≥n-s+1 in T_{n,k,s}, and the one-variable vanishing polynomials place x_i^k in it, so I_{n,k,s}⊆T_{n,k,s}. Lemma 6.4, which is Lemma 3.4 with s replacing k in the elementary-generator cutoff, supplies reverse Demazure characters in I_{n,k,s} with leading skip monomials x(S) for |S|=n-s+1. A comparison with the set M_{n,k,s} gives a Gröbner basis and the standard monomial basis. The cardinality |M_{n,k,s}|=|OP_{n,k,s}| is obtained by relating M_{n,k,s} to M_{n+k-s,k} and using the bijection Ψ from Theorem 4.9.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.8: antisymmetrization factorization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let 1≤j≤k, with the s=0 convention for R_{r,k,0} when j=k. Let ε_j be the antisymmetrization idempotent for the subgroup permuting the last j variables of Q[x1,...,xn]. Let A_{n,k,j} be the alternating subspace of Q[z1,...,zj]/<z1^k,...,zj^k>, whose Hilbert series is q^{binom(j,2)}[k choose j]_q and whose basis is represented by ε_j(z1^{a1}...zj^{aj}) with 0≤a1<...<aj≤k-1. Then, as graded S_{n-j}-modules, ε_j R_{n,k} is isomorphic to R_{n-j,k,k-j} ⊗ A_{n,k,j}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Split variables into y=(x1,...,x_{n-j}) and z=(x_{n-j+1},...,x_n). Filter both ε_j R_{n,k} and R_{n-j,k,k-j}⊗A_{n,k,j} by total z-degree. Multiplication of representatives defines a map from the tensor product to ε_j R_{n,k}. On the associated graded, the elementary generators e_r(y) with r>n-k kill because e_r(y)g(z) differs from e_r(x)g(z) only by terms of higher z-degree, and e_r(x) is zero in R_{n,k}; the variable-power generators also kill. Thus the map factors through R_{n-j,k,k-j}. The authors then compare explicit bases: on the source, use nonskip monomials in y times alternating monomials with strictly increasing z-exponents; on the target, use ε_j of nonskip monomials in all variables whose z-exponents are strictly increasing. Lemma 6.5 identifies these bases, and multiplication sends one basis bijectively to the other on the associated graded, proving the isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.9 and Lemma 6.10: exact sequence and Frobenius expansion for R_{n,k,s}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For s<k≤n there is a short exact sequence of graded S_n-modules 0 → R_{n,k-1,s} → R_{n,k,s} → R_{n,k,s+1} → 0, where the first map is multiplication by e_{n-s}(x) and has degree n-s, and the second map is the quotient projection. Equivalently, grFrob(R_{n,k,s};q) = grFrob(R_{n,k,s+1};q) + q^{n-s} grFrob(R_{n,k-1,s};q). Iterating this recursion gives, for 1≤s≤k≤n, grFrob(R_{n,k,s};q) = ∑_{m=0}^{k-s} q^{m(n-k+m)} [k-s choose m]_q grFrob(R_{n,k-m};q), with the convention that terms with invalid indices vanish. In the indexing used for antisymmetrization, this is equivalent to grFrob(R_{n-j,k,k-j};q) = ∑_{m=max(1,k-j)}^{min(k,n-j)} q^{(k-m)(n-j-m)} [j choose k-m]_q grFrob(R_{n-j,m};q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The projection R_{n,k,s} → R_{n,k,s+1} is induced by the ideal containment I_{n,k,s}⊆I_{n,k,s+1}. For the left map, multiply by e_{n-s}(x). The authors check directly that x_i^{k-1}e_{n-s}(x) is zero modulo I_{n,k,s}: repeatedly expand e_r(x1,...,xn) with respect to x_i and use x_i^k=0 and e_n,e_{n-1},...,e_{n-s+1}=0 to reduce to zero. Thus multiplication factors through R_{n,k-1,s}. Its image is the kernel of the projection, and exactness follows from the dimension identity |OP_{n,k,s}|=|OP_{n,k,s+1}|+|OP_{n,k-1,s}|, obtained by deciding whether the last forced large element forms a singleton final block. The closed formula is proved by induction using this exact sequence and the q-Pascal recursion for q-binomial coefficients.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.11: graded Frobenius character equals the t=0 Delta specialization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For all k≤n, the graded Frobenius character of R_{n,k} is grFrob(R_{n,k};q) = D_{n,k}(x;q), where D_{n,k}(x;q)=rev_q(ω C_{n,k}(x;q)) and C_{n,k}(x;q) is the common t=0 specialization of the combinatorial Delta Conjecture expressions Risen_{n,k} and Val_{n,k}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses Lemma 3.6: it suffices to show that e_j^⊥ has the same effect on grFrob(R_{n,k};q) and on D_{n,k}(x;q) for all j≥1. For the algebraic side, e_j^⊥ grFrob(R_{n,k};q) is the Frobenius character of ε_j R_{n,k}, where ε_j antisymmetrizes the last j letters. Lemma 6.8 identifies ε_j R_{n,k} with R_{n-j,k,k-j}⊗A_{n,k,j}; the alternating factor contributes q^{binom(j,2)}[k choose j]_q. Lemma 6.10 expands grFrob(R_{n-j,k,k-j};q) as a q-binomial sum of grFrob(R_{n-j,m};q). By induction on n, those smaller Frobenius characters are D_{n-j,m}(x;q). The resulting expression is exactly the recurrence for e_j^⊥D_{n,k} in Lemma 3.7. Equality of constant terms is clear, so Lemma 3.6 proves the theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6.12: ordered multiset partition interpretations of grFrob(R_{n,k})
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let OP_{γ,k} be the set of ordered multiset partitions of content γ and k blocks, where γ ranges over weak compositions of n. Then grFrob(R_{n,k};q), after applying ω and q-reversal in the inverse direction, is equal to each of the four ordered multiset partition generating functions ∑_{|γ|=n}∑_{μ in OP_{γ,k}} q^{inv(μ)}x^γ, ∑ q^{maj(μ)}x^γ, ∑ q^{dinv(μ)}x^γ, and ∑ q^{minimaj(μ)}x^γ. Equivalently, grFrob(R_{n,k};q)=rev_q(ω of any of these four expressions).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The four ordered multiset partition expressions are known to be equal to C_{n,k}(x;q), the common t=0 specialization of the two combinatorial Delta Conjecture sides. Theorem 6.11 identifies grFrob(R_{n,k};q) with D_{n,k}=rev_q(ω C_{n,k}). Applying this definition to any of the four expressions yields the corollary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6.13: Schur expansion by standard Young tableaux
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every k≤n, grFrob(R_{n,k};q) = ∑_{T in SYT(n)} q^{maj(T)} [n-des(T)-1 choose n-k]_q s_{shape(T)}(x), where SYT(n) is the set of standard Young tableaux with n boxes, des(T) is the number of descents of T, maj(T) is the sum of descents of T, and s_{shape(T)} is the Schur function of the shape of T. Equivalently, the coefficient of s_μ(x) is ∑_{T in SYT(μ)} q^{maj(T)} [n-des(T)-1 choose n-k]_q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof starts from a theorem of A. T. Wilson giving the Schur expansion of the ordered multiset partition function C_{n,k}(x;q). Taking the coefficient of the relevant parameter in Wilson's formula and then applying ω and rev_q gives an intermediate expression involving q^{binom(n,2)-maj(T)}, [des(T) choose n-k]_q, and s_{shape(T)'}(x). Transpose tableaux convert this to the displayed formula using maj(T')=binom(n,2)-maj(T) and des(T')=n-des(T)-1. This corollary is the paper's most explicit Schur-positive formula for grFrob(R_{n,k}).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.14: dual Hall-Littlewood expansion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Q'_λ(x;q) be the dual Hall-Littlewood symmetric function and let m_i(λ) be the multiplicity of i as a part of λ. Then grFrob(R_{n,k};q) = rev_q[ ∑_{λ partition of n, ℓ(λ)=k} q^{∑_i (i-1)(λ_i-1)} [k; m1(λ),...,mn(λ)]_q Q'_λ(x;q) ], where [k; m1,...,mn]_q is the q-multinomial coefficient.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using Theorem 6.11, it is enough to expand ω C_{n,k}(x;q). At t=0 in the rise version of the Delta expression, only balanced Dyck paths contribute; such paths are indexed by strong compositions α of n. For a fixed balanced Dyck path p(α), the sum over labelings is an LLT/Hall-Littlewood expression: ∑_λ q^{mindinv(p(α))} K_{λ',μ(α)}(q)s_λ(x), where μ(α) is the decreasing rearrangement of α and K is the Kostka-Foulkes coefficient in Q'_{μ(α)}. Grouping all α with the same decreasing rearrangement λ produces the q-multinomial coefficient. The minimum dinv of the reverse composition of λ is ∑_i(i-1)(λ_i-1). Substituting this into the Hall-Littlewood expansion and applying q-reversal gives the stated formula.

proof_techniques.name: Garsia-Procesi point-set degeneration
proof_techniques.description: The paper realizes the desired quotient as a top-degree degeneration of a finite S_n-stable point set. For R_{n,k}, the point set Y_{n,k} consists of n-tuples whose coordinates use exactly k fixed distinct scalars; it is S_n-equivariantly equivalent to ordered set partitions OP_{n,k}. The ideal T(Y) generated by top-degree components of the vanishing ideal has the same dimension and ungraded S_n-module structure as the coordinate ring of the point set. Showing I_{n,k}⊆T(Y_{n,k}) and then proving a matching dimension bound forces equality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook Schur coefficients of a multigraded coinvariant object can be isolated by a degeneration or specialization, a finite point-set model may turn the coefficient problem into a count of labeled combinatorial objects. The technique bypasses regular-sequence arguments, which often fail for generalized coinvariant-type ideals.

proof_techniques.name: Skip monomials and Demazure-character Gröbner bases
proof_techniques.description: The authors introduce skip monomials x(S) and prove that they occur as lexicographic leading terms of carefully chosen reverse Demazure characters lying in the ideal I_{n,k}. The resulting initial ideal is generated by x_i^k together with skip monomials, and its standard monomials are exactly nonskip monomials. A signed Demazure-character identity, proved by a sign-reversing involution on partisan skylines, is the mechanism that places the needed Demazure characters in the ideal.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a multigraded or hook-refined coinvariant algebra, an analogous initial ideal could give a monomial model for coefficients. Skip-type divisibility conditions are especially useful because they naturally encode ordered set partitions and q-binomial factors.

proof_techniques.name: Recursive star/bar insertion preserving a complementary statistic
proof_techniques.description: Ordered set partitions are built by either inserting the largest letter into an existing block, called star insertion, or inserting it as a new singleton block, called bar insertion. The coinv statistic changes by explicitly known increments under these operations. The paper mirrors this recursion on nonskip monomials by either multiplying by a power of the last variable or by multiplying by a squarefree monomial m(S) attached to a uniquely determined skip set S.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients often involve adding a cell to a row or column, or adding an exterior generator. A comparable insertion recursion could produce a direct combinatorial interpretation with weights matching multidegrees or Schur hook parameters.

proof_techniques.name: Frobenius determination by e_j^⊥ and antisymmetrization
proof_techniques.description: A central proof strategy is to show two symmetric functions are equal by proving all their e_j^⊥ images agree. Representation-theoretically, e_j^⊥ corresponds to applying the antisymmetrization idempotent ε_j on j letters and then taking the Frobenius character for the remaining symmetric group. The paper identifies ε_j R_{n,k} with a tensor product R_{n-j,k,k-j}⊗A_{n,k,j}, where A_{n,k,j} is a finite alternating quotient with Hilbert series q^{binom(j,2)}[k choose j]_q.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is highly relevant to hook coefficients, since hook Schur functions are controlled by exterior powers and Pieri operations involving elementary symmetric functions. A solver could try to express hook coefficients c_λ(n) through repeated e_j^⊥ operations or antisymmetric components of a related module.

proof_techniques.name: Auxiliary quotient family and exact sequences
proof_techniques.description: To make the e_j^⊥ recursion close, the paper enlarges the family R_{n,k} to R_{n,k,s}, where only the top s elementary symmetric functions are imposed. Multiplication by e_{n-s} gives a degree n-s map R_{n,k-1,s} → R_{n,k,s}, and quotienting gives R_{n,k,s} → R_{n,k,s+1}; these form a short exact sequence. Iterating the resulting Frobenius recursion expresses each R_{n,k,s} as a q-binomial combination of the original R_{n,m}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the target hook coefficients are not directly recursive, introducing an extra parameter that records how many elementary or exterior conditions have been imposed may produce exact sequences whose graded Euler characteristics yield the desired coefficients.

proof_techniques.name: Gessel fundamental expansion via standardization of ordered multiset partitions
proof_techniques.description: The paper converts ordered multiset partition generating functions into sums of Gessel fundamental quasisymmetric functions by standardizing reading words. This bridges raw combinatorial statistics and symmetric-function Schur expansions, because Schur functions decompose positively into fundamentals over standard Young tableaux with fixed descent sets. The involution ω is handled combinatorially by reversing reading words.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients can often be extracted from fundamental expansions by restricting to standard tableaux of hook shape or to descent sets with hook-compatible structure. This provides a plausible route from a multigraded Hilbert series to a concrete descent-set enumeration.

proof_techniques.name: Straightening against generalized Garsia-Stanton monomials
proof_techniques.description: The generalized Garsia-Stanton basis is proved using a straightening relation that rewrites any monomial as a Garsia-Stanton monomial times elementary symmetric functions, modulo lower terms in a dominance/inversion order. The generators of I_{n,k} kill the terms with too many descents or too large an elementary factor, leaving precisely the generalized basis monomials. Their degree generating function matches a major-index/q-binomial formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the hook coefficient problem can be reduced to a quotient where elementary symmetric functions vanish, a similar straightening algorithm may produce a basis indexed by permutations or tableaux with descent restrictions, which are well suited to hook-shaped Schur enumerations.

proof_techniques.name: Schur expansion through tableau statistics
proof_techniques.description: The final Schur formula expresses grFrob(R_{n,k}) as a sum over all standard Young tableaux T weighted by q^{maj(T)} and a q-binomial depending only on des(T). This packages the representation-theoretic multiplicity of each irreducible Specht module into a purely tableau-theoretic generating function.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook partitions λ, this formula specializes immediately to a weighted enumeration of standard Young tableaux of hook shape λ. Even if the original algebra differs from R_{n,k}, this suggests that hook coefficients should be sought as major-index/descent enumerators over hook tableaux or equivalent descent words.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not state a hook-only corollary, but Corollary 6.13 gives the coefficient of every Schur function s_μ in grFrob(R_{n,k};q), so specializing μ to a hook shape gives an immediate hook-shaped tableau interpretation in the R_{n,k} setting. The ordered multiset partition formulas used in Corollary 6.12 are imported from Haglund-Remmel-Wilson, Wilson, and Rhoades; those cited works may contain additional hook-relevant refinements of inv, maj, dinv, and minimaj. The dual Hall-Littlewood expansion in Theorem 6.14 connects R_{n,k} to Springer/Tanisaki-style modules; the paper's Problem 7.1 asks for a direct algebraic relationship between I_{n,k} and Tanisaki ideals. The authors emphasize that R_{n,k} gives a singly graded module for the t=0 Delta specialization, while finding a full bigraded module for the Delta Conjecture remains open in the paper. Notational caution: the x alphabet in Frobenius and Schur formulas is a symmetric-function alphabet for S_n-characters, whereas q is the grading variable; this differs from the original problem's use of q_1,...,q_m as multigrading variables.
why_relevant: Constructs generalized coinvariant algebras whose combinatorics are controlled by ordered set partitions and identifies their Frobenius series with a t=0 Delta specialization. Ordered set partitions are a natural combinatorial model for the superspace Hilbert and Frobenius series relevant to hook coefficients.
